diagrams.title: Android Project Directory Structure
diagrams.description: A hierarchical tree diagram showing the complete folder structure of a newly created Android project in Android Studio, with expandable sections for app, gradle, and configuration files
elements: Root folder icon with project name
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand where files are located and their roles in the project, reducing confusion when navigating real Android projects

diagrams.title: Android Application Build Process
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing the complete compilation pipeline from source code to APK, with arrows indicating the sequence and transformation at each stage
elements: Arrow to: Java Compiler → Bytecode (.class files)
Arrow to: DEX Compiler → Dalvik Executable (classes.dex)
Arrow to: APK Packager → Final APK (Android Package)
Parallelogram boxes: Android SDK Libraries, AAPT (Android Asset Packaging Tool)
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the multi-step compilation process, helping students understand how their code and resources transform into a deployable Android application

diagrams.title: Activity-Layout Relationship Diagram
diagrams.description: A diagram showing the connection between the Activity Java/Kotlin file and the XML layout file, with arrows depicting the data flow and method calls
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the fundamental relationship between Java/Kotlin code and XML layouts, a core concept for all Android UI development

diagrams.title: Android Studio IDE Main Window Layout
diagrams.description: A labeled wireframe of Android Studio showing the key panels and windows a developer sees when creating the first app
elements: Top bar: Menu Bar (File, Edit, View, Build, Run)
Below top: Toolbar with Run (green arrow), Build, Debug buttons
Bottom bar: Build Output console, Terminal
Far right: AVD Manager button (device icon)
diagrams.purpose: Orients students to the Android Studio interface, identifying key areas they will interact with during development

diagrams.title: Run Configuration and Deployment Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing how a developer runs the app from Android Studio to either emulator or physical device
elements: Start box: Developer clicks 'Run' button (green triangle)
Arrow to: Select Deployment Target dialog
Decision diamond: Choose Emulator or Physical Device?
Path A (Emulator): AVD Manager → Launch Emulator → Install APK → Run
Path B (USB Debugging): Detect connected device via ADB → Install APK → Run
End box: App displays on selected target device
diagrams.purpose: Explains the deployment process, helping students understand the options for testing their applications

charts.title: Android Emulator vs Physical Device Comparison
charts.content: A two-column comparison table with rows: Performance (Emulator: slower, Device: faster), Hardware Access (Emulator: limited to sensors, Device: full access), Debugging (Emulator: easy with logs, Device: real-time), Setup (Emulator: requires AVD config, Device: requires USB drivers), Use Case (Emulator: testing layouts, Device: testing hardware features)

charts.title: Step-by-Step: Create First Android App
charts.content: Vertical flowchart with 8 steps: 1. Open Android Studio → 2. Select 'New Project' → 3. Choose 'Empty Views Activity' template → 4. Name project (e.g., MyFirstApp) → 5. Select language (Kotlin/Java) and minimum SDK → 6. Wait for Gradle sync to complete → 7. Click Run (green arrow) → 8. Select emulator or connected device

charts.title: Key Files in Your First Android Project
charts.content: Three-column table with columns: File Name, Location, Purpose. Rows: MainActivity.kt (app/src/main/java/, Entry point - onCreate method), activity_main.xml (app/src/main/res/layout/, Defines UI layout), AndroidManifest.xml (app/src/main/, Declares app components & permissions), build.gradle (app/, Defines dependencies & SDK versions), strings.xml (app/src/main/res/values/, Stores string resources)

charts.title: Android Application Components Classification
charts.content: A classification chart showing four main Android app components: Activity (UI, one screen), Service (background operations), BroadcastReceiver (responds to system events), ContentProvider (shares data between apps). For beginner level, focus on Activity as the main component

branches: Development Environment: Android Studio IDE, JDK, Android SDK
Project Structure: app module, source files, resources, manifest
Build Process: Compile → DEX → Package → APK
Execution: Run button → Select target → Install → Launch
Testing Targets: Android Emulator (AVD), Physical Device (USB debugging)
mindMaps.purpose: Provides a comprehensive overview connecting all elements of creating the first app, helping students see how the pieces fit together before diving into detailed implementation
